schools.summary: Matthew Henry reads Solomon's wisdom as given for action: he builds first a house for God's name, then a palace for the kingdom's good — 'we are not born for ourselves, but for God and our country.'
Clarke and Wesley divide the two houses — a temple for Jehovah's worship and a royal palace for Solomon and his successors.
Gill reads the resolve to build for the name of the LORD as a purpose of worship, to which David had directed and encouraged his son.
Ellicott parses 'determined' as literally 'said' — here purposed or resolved (cf. 1 Kings 5:5) — and 'house for his kingdom' as a palace whose building Chronicles never relates.

Keil and Delitzsch frame the whole chapter — the numbering of workmen, the treaty with Hiram, the repeated levy — and note the Chronicler omits Hiram's congratulatory embassy; Ellicott marks the verse as the hinge into the treaty with Huram.
Gill flags the difference in the overseers' number from the parallel and defers its solution to the fuller notice.

Ellicott sets the embassy against 1 Kings 5:2-11 (Huram had first congratulated Solomon), notes the spellings Hiram/Hirom and the later Tyrian king of that name under Tiglath-pileser II, and the supplied 'even so deal with me.'
Gill notes Huram is the same as Hiram and that a letter of congratulation had already passed from Tyre, unrecorded here.
Clarke treats the differing spellings Chiram/Churam as one name, only a yod and vau interchanged.

themes: a friendship reaching back to David

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott unfold the temple's appointed worship — incense (Exod. 30:7), continual shewbread, burnt offerings of sabbaths, new moons, and set feasts (Num. 28) — and read 'this is an ordinance for ever' as the standing 'statute for ever' of the law.
Gill reads the opening as Solomon's fixed and determined resolve to build.

Keil and Delitzsch ground the house's greatness in God's greatness above all gods, yet insist the heaven of heavens cannot contain him (cf. Solomon's prayer, 1 Kings 8:27); Ellicott adds that by modern notions the temple was in fact a small building.
Gill: the temple was not so very large but magnificent in structure and decoration.
Wesley: the temple strictly so called was small, but its attendant buildings were large and numerous.

themes: great is our God above all gods

schools.summary: Clarke expands the thought with the Targum (the three heavens cannot contain him) and Wesley with the warning not to comprehend the infinite God within the house; both make the temple's end the offering of sacrifice, not God's containment.

Ellicott renders 'who could keep strength?' and hears Solomon's prayer echoed (1 Kings 8:27) — the house is not for God's residence but his service; Keil and Delitzsch take 'to burn incense' as a part for the whole worship.
Gill: who is able to build a house suitable to God's majesty, since he dwells not in temples made with hands?

themes: the heaven of heavens cannot contain him
a house for worship not habitation

schools.summary: Ellicott unfolds the craftsman's versatility — gold, silver, purple (Aramaic form), crimson (Persian, cf. 'carmine'), blue, and carving in wood or stone — to labour with the men David had provided.
Gill grounds the request for a metalworker in the many temple vessels of gold, silver, brass, and iron.
Clarke: Solomon asks for a man of great ingenuity, able to plan and direct, set over the other artists.

Ellicott takes 'algum' as sandal wood and flags a difficulty — it is sought 'out of Lebanon' though it came from Ophir (2 Chron. 9:10) — suspecting an inaccuracy or an ideal sketch; Keil and Delitzsch read the syntax of the shared labour and Solomon's promised provisions.
Gill identifies algum with almug (letters transposed), ruling out coral and Brazil-wood, and takes the word as Arabic.

Ellicott reads 'let them prepare' (the Chronicler's favoured infinitive) and 'wonderful great' as the LXX's 'great and glorious'; Keil and Delitzsch make the abundance of timber the ground of the request and pass on to the wages and Hiram's reply.
Gill grounds the abundance of timber in the raftering, ceiling, wainscoting, and flooring of so vast a house.
Ellicott recasts the verse to harmonize with 1 Kings 5:11 (provisions for Hiram's household), argues 'beaten wheat' is a misreading for 'food,' and glosses the kor and bath (cf. Luke 16:6-7).
Gill weighs 'beaten wheat' as fine flour or food, paid so because wheat was scarce among the Tyrians.

debates.question: Were the great stores of grain, wine, and oil the wages of the Tyrian woodcutters, or the yearly provision of Hiram's royal household?

sides.position: the wages of the hewers named in the verse
provision for Hiram's royal household, harmonizing with 1 Kings 5:11
two distinct agreements, one for the household and one for the workmen

themes: wages of grain, wine, and oil

schools.summary: Matthew Henry draws from Huram's 'because the LORD loved his people' the lesson that a good government is a token of God's favour and that princes are preferred for the public good, not their own.
Clarke turns 'answered in writing' into a reflection on literacy — these 'barbarous' courts could write, while a later British king could not sign his name.
Ellicott reads 'said in a letter' as implying Solomon's message was oral, and notes that in the parallel Huram blesses Jehovah before writing.
Gill: in his letter Huram granted Solomon all that he desired.

Ellicott notes the fuller ascription to the Maker of heaven and earth is peculiar to Chronicles (1 Kings 5:7 is briefer) and may be the Chronicler's monotheistic shaping, though a Phoenician might know the title (cf. Gen. 14:19); Keil and Delitzsch simply follow the letter's course.
Wesley judges Huram a proselyte who worshipped Jehovah as the Maker of heaven and earth and fountain of being.
Gill: Huram had some good notions of God as the Former and Maker of all things.

debates.question: Does Huram's blessing of 'the LORD... that made heaven and earth' express his own conversion, or the Chronicler's shaping of his words?

sides.position: the genuine confession of a proselyte who worshipped Jehovah
the Chronicler shaped his source in a monotheistic direction, though the title may have been known to the Phoenician

themes: blessed be the Maker of heaven and earth

schools.summary: Ellicott reads 'Huram my father' as a title of honour (master, preceptor), the craftsman being called Solomon's 'father' at 2 Chron. 4:16 (cf. Gen. 45:8; Judg. 17:10).
Clarke supplies the craftsman's name as Hiram, or Hiram Abi, from 1 Kings 7:13-14.
Gill dwells on the man's fitness — endued with understanding for the very tasks Solomon required.

debates.question: Is 'Huram-abi' the craftsman's proper name or an honorific title?

sides.position: 'abi' means 'my father,' a title of a master or preceptor, the man named simply Huram

schools.summary: Ellicott lays out the Dan/Naphtali discrepancy, offering both Bertheau's harmony (a Danite widowed in Naphtali, then wed to a Tyrian) and a possible textual corruption; he assigns 'skilful' to Huram, not his father, and notes Chronicles enlarges the artist's gifts beyond the 'worker in brass' of Kings.
Gill flags the seeming disagreement with 1 Kings 7:14 and defers to his reconciliation there.

debates.question: How is the mother 'of the daughters of Dan' (Chronicles) reconciled with 'of the tribe of Naphtali' (1 Kings 7:14)?

sides.position: a Danite by birth who married into Naphtali, was widowed, then wed a Tyrian — so both tribes claim her
'daughters of Dan' is a textual corruption of 'Naphtali'

themes: Jew and Gentile in one craftsman

schools.summary: Ellicott reads Huram's plain acceptance of the exchange, taking 'his servants' as himself and his court, the correlative of 'lord.'
Gill weighs 'my lord' — whether it marks Huram as tributary to Solomon or is only a respectful form of speech, and leans to the latter.

Ellicott parses the emphatic 'we,' the rare words for 'need' and 'floats' (rafts, cf. 1 Kings 5:9), and Joppa as the harbour nearest Jerusalem, concluding the Chronicler knew and expanded the older text, though neither preserves the original documents.
Gill notes the wood is both cedar and fir, as Solomon had asked.
Clarke points to his fuller notes at 1 Kings 5:9 and the parallel places.

Ellicott takes 'the strangers' as the indigenous Canaanites (cf. Gen. 23:4), liable to forced labour already under David (1 Chron. 22:2; 2 Sam. 20:24), and sums the total 153,600 as the figures of the next verse.
Gill identifies the strangers as the Canaanite remnant, now proselytes of the gate rather than idolaters.
Wesley explains the fresh count: David had numbered them, but death required Solomon to number them again.

Ellicott checks the numbers — 70,000 and 80,000 agree with 1 Kings 5:15, but 3,600 overseers against 3,300 (three and six confused, the Chronicler right) — and notes the omitted levy of 30,000 Israelites; Keil and Delitzsch give the plain distribution.
Gill repeats the count and reads the levy of the nations as an emblem of the Gentiles building the spiritual temple, the church.
Wesley: Solomon would not put the free-born Israelites to this drudgery, but the landless proselyte strangers.

themes: the Gentiles and the spiritual temple
